transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Caleb. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been feeling a bit stuck lately, especially with my PhD project. It just seems like I should be further along by now, you know? It’s hard to shake off this feeling that I’m not doing enough.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of pressure regarding your progress on your PhD project, and that feeling of being stuck is weighing on you. Can you tell me more about what specifically feels challenging right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... the last part of the project feels really overwhelming. I keep thinking about how much I still have left to do, and it feels like this mountain I can’t climb. I try to sit down and work, but then I get distracted by other things, like painting or just... anything else, really. It’s like I’m avoiding it, but I don’t want to be.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the final part of your project feels daunting, almost like an insurmountable challenge. I hear you saying that you find yourself getting distracted and avoiding the work, even though you don't want to. That must be frustrating. What do you think is behind that urge to avoid it?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it is frustrating. I think part of it is just this fear of not meeting my own expectations. I keep telling myself I should have finished by now, and when I sit down to work, it just feels like I can’t do it. I guess that fear makes it easier to just... not do anything at all. It’s like I’m caught in this loop of anxiety and avoidance.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that fear of not meeting your expectations is really powerful for you, and it creates a cycle of anxiety and avoidance. It must be tough to feel caught in that loop. Can you share more about the expectations you have for yourself? Where do you think those expectations come from?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think a lot of it comes from wanting to prove to myself that I can do this, you know? I’ve always been someone who wants to achieve and do well, and now that I’m so close, it feels like I can’t mess it up. Maybe there’s also some pressure from outside—like what others might think if I don’t finish on time. It’s hard to shake off that feeling that I should be further along.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there's a strong drive within you to achieve and prove your capabilities, which is understandable, especially when you're so close to finishing. The added pressure of external expectations can make it even more challenging. It seems like you're balancing both your own aspirations and the perceptions of others. How does that pressure from outside influence your feelings about your progress?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It definitely adds to the anxiety. I find myself worrying about what others might think if I don’t finish on time or if my work isn’t up to par. It’s like I’m constantly measuring myself against some invisible standard. That makes it hard to focus on my own journey and what I want to achieve. Instead of feeling proud of what I’ve done so far, I just feel this weight of expectation that makes me want to retreat even more.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that invisible standard creates a heavy weight on your shoulders, making it difficult to appreciate your own progress and accomplishments. It’s understandable that this pressure would lead you to want to retreat rather than move forward. How do you feel about the idea of shifting your focus from those external expectations to your own journey and what you want to achieve?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think that could be really helpful, but it’s easier said than done. I mean, I know I should focus on my own journey, but it’s hard to let go of those external pressures. I worry that if I don’t measure up to what others expect, then maybe I’m not good enough. It’s like I’m stuck between wanting to be proud of my work and feeling like I have to meet these standards. I guess I just don’t know how to make that shift without feeling lost.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that letting go of those external pressures feels challenging, especially when they’re tied to your sense of self-worth. It sounds like you’re navigating a complex mix of wanting to feel proud of your work while also grappling with the fear of not measuring up. That can create a lot of inner conflict. What do you think might help you start to explore your own standards and values, separate from those external expectations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe taking some time to reflect on what success really means to me could help. I’ve been so focused on what I think I should achieve that I haven’t really thought about what I actually want from this experience. Maybe journaling or even talking it out could help clarify my thoughts. But then again, there’s that fear of realizing I’m not where I want to be, and that makes me hesitant to dive into it. It’s like I want to explore, but I’m scared of what I might find.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have a sense that reflecting on your own definition of success could be beneficial, but the fear of confronting where you are versus where you want to be is holding you back. That hesitation is completely understandable. Exploring those feelings can be daunting, especially when there’s a fear of what you might discover. What do you think would feel safe or manageable about starting that exploration, even if it’s just a small step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe starting with small, manageable questions could help. Like, what do I enjoy about my research? Or what parts of the process make me feel fulfilled? I think if I focus on the aspects that I genuinely like, it might make it easier to separate my own feelings from those external pressures. I could even set aside a little time just to think about those things without any pressure to come up with answers right away. It feels less intimidating that way, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach, Caleb. Starting with small, manageable questions can create a space for you to explore what you genuinely enjoy about your research without the weight of external expectations. Setting aside time to reflect without pressure to find immediate answers could help you connect with your own feelings and motivations. How do you think you might feel after giving yourself that space to explore those questions?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it could feel refreshing, maybe even a bit liberating. Just having that time to focus on what I enjoy might help me reconnect with my passion for the work. It could also give me a clearer perspective on what I really want to achieve, rather than just what I think I should achieve. I guess there’s a part of me that hopes it might ease some of that pressure I’ve been feeling. But I’m still a bit nervous about it, too.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s great to hear that you see the potential for it to feel refreshing and liberating. Reconnecting with your passion could indeed help ease some of that pressure. It’s completely natural to feel nervous about this exploration, especially when it involves confronting your feelings and expectations. What do you think might help you manage that nervousness as you take this step towards reconnecting with what you enjoy?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think reminding myself that it’s okay to feel nervous could help. Maybe I could approach it with curiosity instead of pressure, like it’s just an exploration rather than a test. I could also set a timer for those reflection sessions, so it feels contained and not overwhelming. Having a supportive friend or colleague to talk to about it afterward might help too, just to share what I discover without judgment. That way, it feels less isolating.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like wonderful strategies, Caleb. Approaching it with curiosity and allowing yourself to feel nervous without judgment can create a more open and supportive environment for your exploration. Setting a timer to keep those reflection sessions contained is a practical way to manage any overwhelm. Having someone to share your discoveries with can also provide a sense of connection and support, which is really valuable. How do you feel about taking these steps in the coming days?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful about it, actually. Having a plan makes it seem less daunting. I think I can give it a try and see how it goes. Just knowing that I can approach it with curiosity and that I have support if I need it makes it feel more manageable. I’m still a little nervous, but I think that’s okay. It feels like a step in the right direction.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m glad to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful and that having a plan makes it feel more manageable. It’s completely okay to feel a bit nervous; it shows that you’re stepping outside your comfort zone, which can lead to growth. Taking that step in the right direction is significant. As you move forward with this exploration, remember to be gentle with yourself and celebrate any insights you gain along the way. Is there anything else on your mind today that you’d like to discuss or explore further?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ve covered a lot of what’s been on my mind today. It feels good to have a clearer sense of direction and some strategies to try. I appreciate the space to talk about it. I guess I just want to make sure I keep this momentum going and not fall back into that cycle of avoidance. Maybe we can touch on that next time?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you found our conversation helpful and that you have a clearer sense of direction. It’s completely understandable to want to maintain that momentum and avoid falling back into old patterns. We can definitely explore that further next time. Thank you for sharing your thoughts and feelings today, Caleb. I look forward to continuing this journey with you. Take care until we talk again!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I appreciate your support and understanding. I’ll do my best to keep that momentum going. Looking forward to our next conversation. Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're very welcome, Caleb! I'm glad to be here for you. Wishing you all the best as you take those steps forward. Take care, and I look forward to our next conversation as well!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! That means a lot. I’ll take care and see you next time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're welcome, Caleb! I'm glad to hear that. Take care of yourself, and I'll be here whenever you're ready to talk again. See you next time!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: See you next time! Take care!
